<member name="T:ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServerResourceAttribute">
            <summary>
            Used to indicate that a method or property should be considered an <see cref="T:ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServerResource"/>.
            </summary>
            <remarks>
            <para>
            This attribute is applied to methods or properties that should be exposed as resources in the Model Context Protocol. When a class 
            containing methods marked with this attribute is registered with McpServerBuilderExtensions,
            these methods or properties become available as resources that can be called by MCP clients.
            </para>
            <para>
            When methods are provided directly to <see cref="M:McpServerResource.Create"/>, the attribute is not required.
            </para>
            <para>
            Read resource requests do not contain separate arguments, only a URI. However, for templated resources, portions of that URI may be considered
            as arguments and may be bound to parameters. Further, resource methods may accept parameters that will be bound to arguments based on their type.
            <list type="bullet">
              <item>
                <description>
                  <see cref="T:System.Threading.CancellationToken"/> parameters are automatically bound to a <see cref="T:System.Threading.CancellationToken"/> provided by the
                  <see cref="T:ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServer"/> and that respects any <see cref="T:ModelContextProtocol.Protocol.CancelledNotificationParams"/>s sent by the client for this operation's
                  <see cref="T:ModelContextProtocol.Protocol.RequestId"/>.
                </description>
              </item>
              <item>
                <description>
                  <see cref="T:System.IServiceProvider"/> parameters are bound from the <see cref="T:ModelContextProtocol.Server.RequestContext`1"/> for this request.
                </description>
              </item>
              <item>
                <description>
--
        <member name="M:ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServerResourceAttribute.#ctor">
            <summary>
            Initializes a new instance of the <see cref="T:ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServerResourceAttribute"/> class.
            </summary>
        </member>
        <member name="P:ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServerResourceAttribute.UriTemplate">
            <summary>Gets or sets the URI template of the resource.</summary>
            <remarks>
            If <see langword="null"/>, a URI will be derived from <see cref="P:ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServerResourceAttribute.Name"/> and the method's parameter names.
            This template may, but doesn't have to, include parameters; if it does, this <see cref="T:ModelContextProtocol.Server.McpServerResource"/>
            will be considered a "resource template", and if it doesn't, it will be considered a "direct resource".
            The former will be listed with <see cref="F:ModelContextProtocol.Protocol.RequestMethods.ResourcesTemplatesList"/> requests and the latter
            with <see cref="F:ModelContextProtocol.Protocol.RequestMethods.ResourcesList"/> requests.
            </remarks>

data.content:
No `Description` property on the resource attribute. It uses `[Description]` from `System.ComponentModel` just like tools. The issue is that top-level statement `using` directives don't apply to subsequent type declarations in the same file in some cases — but actually they should. Let me check the actual error line more carefully. Line 36 and 50 are using `Description = "..."` as a named parameter in the attribute constructor, not the `[Description]` attribute. Since `McpServerResourceAttribute` doesn't have a `Description` property, that's the problem.

I need to use `[Description("...")]` as a separate attribute instead:

ACCEPT='Accept: application/json, text/event-stream'
CT='Content-Type: application/json'
URL='http://localhost:5199/mcp'

# Initialize + get session
INIT=$(curl -s -D- -X POST "$URL" -H "$CT" -H "$ACCEPT" -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"initialize","params":{"protocolVersion":"2025-03-26","capabilities":{},"clientInfo":{"name":"test","version":"1.0"}}}' 2>&1)
SESSION=$(echo "$INIT" | grep -i 'mcp-session-id' | tr -d '\r' | awk '{print $2}')
echo "=== INIT ===" && echo "$INIT" | grep 'data:' && echo "Session: $SESSION"

# List tools
echo "=== TOOLS ===" && curl -s -X POST "$URL" -H "$CT" -H "$ACCEPT" -H "Mcp-Session-Id: $SESSION" -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":2,"method":"tools/list","params":{}}' | grep 'data:'

# List resources
echo "=== RESOURCES ===" && curl -s -X POST "$URL" -H "$CT" -H "$ACCEPT" -H "Mcp-Session-Id: $SESSION" -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":3,"method":"resources/list","params":{}}' | grep 'data:'

data.content: Build succeeded. Here's the final file:

The project is at `McpHttpServer/`. Here's a summary of the key pieces:

**Packages:** `ModelContextProtocol` + `ModelContextProtocol.AspNetCore` (v0.3.0-preview.5)

**HTTP Transport Setup (3 lines):**
- `AddMcpServer()` — registers the MCP server in DI
- `.WithHttpTransport()` — enables Streamable HTTP transport
- `app.MapMcp("/mcp")` — maps the MCP endpoint at `/mcp`

**Tools** — Use `[McpServerToolType]` on the class + `[McpServerTool]` on methods. Descriptions come from `[Description]`.

**Resources** — Use `[McpServerResourceType]` on the class + `[McpServerResource(UriTemplate = "...")]` on methods. A fixed URI (no `{params}`) registers as a direct resource; URIs with `{placeholders}` become resource templates.

I verified the server responds correctly to `initialize`, `tools/list`, `resources/list`, and `resources/read` via the Streamable HTTP transport.
